04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine project CIN2 / Ella Anttila

‹#›

PROJECT CIN 2

Lab meeting 4.11.2025

Speaker notes:
Good afternoon everyone. My name is Ella  Today I’ll give an update on our Project CIN 2. This is part of our ongoing work focusing on cervical intraepithelial neoplasia grade 2, or so called CIN2, and how we can better characterize these lesions using different imaging and molecular methods.

AGENDA

04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine Project CIN2 / Ella Anttila

‹#›

Speaker notes:
So Here’s the outline for today’s presentation. I’ll start with some background information to set the context of the study. Then I’ll explain the AIMS of the project — what we wanted to achieve. After that, I’ll describe the cohort, including key characteristics. Next, I’ll give a brief project overview, summarizing the workflow and the methods we’re using. We’ll then go through specific parts of the analysis — the H&E model, followed by t-CyCIF and GeoMX data. Finally, I’ll end with the next steps — what’s coming next in the project and future directions.”

Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia 
Precancerous lesion in the cervix
Caused by persistent HPV infection

04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine Project CIN2 / Ella Anttila

‹#›

WHAT IS CIN2?

Speaker notes:
Before going into the details of the project, I’ll briefly explain what CIN2 means. CIN stands for Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia, which refers to abnormal cell changes in the epithelial layer of the cervix.
It is considered a precancerous lesion in the cervix.
These lesions are mainly caused by a persistent infection with high-risk types of HPV — the human papillomavirus.

Represents moderate dysplasia
Contains a mixture of CIN1-like (HPV-producing) and CIN3-like (transformed) lesions
The underlying biology remains unclear

04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine Project CIN2/ Ella Anttila

‹#›

WHAT IS CIN2?

Speaker notes:
To understand CIN2 a bit better — CIN, or Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia, is divided into grades 1, 2, and 3. CIN2 represents moderate dysplasia.
This means that the cells in the cervical epithelium start to look abnormal, but they haven’t yet invaded deeper tissues.
What makes CIN2 especially interesting is that it’s quite mixed biologically — it often contains both CIN1-like areas, which are still producing HPV, and CIN3-like areas, which are transformed and no longer produce the virus.
Because of this mix, the underlying biology of CIN2 isn’t fully understood. Some lesions actually regress on their own, while others progress toward CIN3 and, in some cases, cervical cancer.
And that uncertainty is one of the key reasons behind our project — to better understand the molecular and spatial features that distinguish CIN2 lesions that regress from those that progress.

04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine Project CIN2 / Ella Anttila

‹#›

Speaker notes:
Next, I’ll briefly go over the current management of CIN2 lesions.
The standard treatment is the LEEP procedure, or Loop Electrosurgical Excision Procedure, which removes abnormal cervical tissue using a low-voltage electrical current — as shown here on the left.
Although LEEP is effective, it can be associated with complications, such as bleeding, infection, and even pregnancy-related complications later on.
What makes CIN2 particularly challenging is that around 50 to 60 percent of these lesions regress spontaneously, especially in younger women under 30. Because of this, active surveillance — meaning careful follow-up without immediate surgery — can be a reasonable management option for selected patients.
This clinical uncertainty — whether to treat or to observe — highlights the need for better biomarkers and molecular understanding of CIN2, which is exactly what our project aims to address.”

A I M :
Integrating Histopathology, Spatial Proteomics, and Transcriptomics to Uncover Mechanisms of Regression in CIN2

04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine Project CIN2 / Ella Anttila

‹#›

Speaker notes:
Based on the uncertainty around CIN2 — where some lesions regress and others progress — the main aim of this project is to uncover the mechanisms of regression in CIN2.
To do this, we’re combining three complementary approaches:
Histopathology, to study the tissue morphology and cellular context;
Spatial proteomics, to analyze protein expression and localization in the tissue microenvironment; and
Transcriptomics, to explore gene expression patterns that might distinguish regressing from persistent lesions.
By integrating these layers of data, we hope to gain a more comprehensive understanding of what drives regression — and ultimately identify potential biomarkers to guide clinical decision-making.

Clinical prospective trial
258 women under 31 with CIN2
Treated with active surveillance
Follow-up time 24 months
colposcopy, cytology, and biopsies every 6 months

04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine Project CIN2 / Ella Anttila

‹#›

COHORT

Bergqvist et al 2025

Speaker notes:
Our study is based on a clinical prospective trial that followed 258 women under the age of 31 diagnosed with CIN2.
Instead of undergoing immediate treatment, these women were managed with active surveillance, meaning that they were monitored carefully without surgical removal of the lesion.
The follow-up period was 24 months, during which participants underwent colposcopy, cytology, and biopsies every six months to evaluate how their lesions changed over time.
As shown in the flowchart, we observed different outcomes — around 64% of the lesions regressed, about 14% showed partial regression, and around 16% progressed to more severe disease.

PROJECT WORKFLOW

04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine Project CIN2/ Ella Anttila

‹#›

Speaker notes:
Here’s an overview of the project workflow, showing how we process and analyze the CIN2 tissue samples.
All our analyses start from the same set of tissue slides, which are then used for three complementary approaches.
First, We developed an AI-based model to identify features associated with regression from Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) images, extracting features such as neoplastic nuclear morphology and immune cell clustering.
Then, we apply t-CyCIF, which allows us to visualize and quantify dozens of protein markers in the same tissue section at single-cell resolution. 
Finally, we use GeoMX spatial transcriptomics to measure gene expression in selected regions of interest, identified based on the H&E and t-CyCIF data.
Together, these methods give us a multi-layered view of each lesion — combining morphology, protein expression, and gene expression — which will help us understand the mechanisms of regression in CIN2.”

H&E PIPELINE

04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine Project CIN2/ Ella Anttila

‹#›

Speaker notes:
We start with H&E-stained slides of CIN2 tissue. Our co-author Oskari Lehtonen from Sampsa´s group developed an AI-based segmentation model that automatically identifies different tissue components and quantifies their features. The model has been successful in predicting which lesions are likely to regress, and it also guides region selection for our  GeoMX analyses.

H&E MODEL RESULTS

04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine Project CIN2/ Ella Anttila

‹#›

Speaker notes:
Here are the top ten features identified by Oskari´s model as predictive of CIN2 regression within two years.
Several interesting patterns emerged. First, smaller colposcopic lesion size and lower intensity of neoplastic nuclei were both associated with regression — suggesting that less severe morphological changes may predict a favorable outcome.
Second, features related to the immune microenvironment appeared repeatedly. For example, a higher proportion of immune cells within the lesion and a greater number of immune cell clusters were predictive of regression, indicating that a more active immune response could support lesion clearance.

04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine Project CIN2 / Ella Anttila

‹#›

Speaker notes:
Next, I’ll go through our t-CyCIF workflow, which we use to characterize the cellular and immune composition of the CIN2 lesions in more detail.
We analyzed a total of 65 samples, divided into different outcome groups — including non-regressors, slow- and quick-regressors, and a few carcinoma and progressor samples for comparison.
Using t-CyCIF, we can stain the same tissue section for dozens of markers through repeated rounds of labeling and imaging.
The panel includes markers for different cell lineages — such as epithelial, immune, and stromal cells — as well as functional markers, including proliferation (Ki-67), immune checkpoints (PD-1, PD-L1), HPV-related proteins (E4), and antigen presentation (HLA).
The images are processed through a pipeline involving illumination correction, stitching with ASHLAR, segmentation, and quantification and CyLinter for quality control and data analysis.
This gives us single-cell–level data describing both the tissue architecture and the functional state of the cells, allowing us to compare regressing and persistent lesions in unprecedented detail.

04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine Project CIN2/ Ella Anttila

‹#›

Speaker notes:
Finally, this slide shows our GeoMX spatial transcriptomics workflow, which we use to study gene expression in selected tissue regions.
We selected the samples based on the H&E results — choosing regressor samples that showed the strongest predictive features of regression, and progressor samples that showed the least of these features. We also included all lesions that progressed into invasive cancer, as well as carcinoma samples for comparison.
Using H&E images together with t-CyCIF guidance, we selected specific regions of interest for profiling on the GeoMX platform, allowing us to measure gene expression while preserving spatial context.
The ROIs were chosen to capture key tissue compartments — including the lesion–stroma interface, normal epithelium, lesion areas, immune cell clusters, and normal stroma.
In total, we analyzed 243 regions of interest and 374 areas of illumination, covering both lesion and stromal compartments.
This approach allows us to link gene expression patterns with the morphological and proteomic features from the H&E and t-CyCIF data, providing a more complete picture of CIN2 biology.”

1. TRIBUS Results
Awaiting data on protein expression
Will complement t-CyCIF findings

2. Integrated Analysis
Combine histopathology, spatial proteomics, and transcriptomics
Identify patterns linked to regression and persistence

3. GeoMX Sequencing & Processing
Generate spatial transcriptomic profiles
Focus on selected CIN2 samples

04/11/2025

Faculty of Medicine Project CIN2/ Ella Anttila

‹#›

NEXT STEPS

Speaker notes:
To finish, here are our next steps for the project.
First, we’re waiting for the TRIBUS results, which will provide additional data on protein expression and complement our t-CyCIF analysis.
Next, we’ll focus on integrating and analyzing all datasets — combining histopathology, spatial proteomics, and transcriptomics — to identify patterns that correlate with regression and persistence.
Finally, we’re moving forward with the GeoMX sequencing and data processing, which will give us the spatial transcriptomic profiles for our selected samples.
Together, these next steps will bring us closer to our main goal — understanding the biological mechanisms behind CIN2 regression and identifying potential biomarkers to support more personalized clinical management.”
